target.name: UCS1025A
target.motivation: UCS1025A is a fungal alkaloid with anti-tumor and antibiotic activity. The authors aim to achieve a formal total synthesis using a novel intramolecular imide addition to establish stereochemistry, avoiding chiral pool or resolution strategies.

success_criteria.free_text: Formal total synthesis of UCS1025A, establishing the stereochemistry of the pyrrolizidine fragment.

key_disconnections.bond_description: C-C bond formation to form the cyclic acetal
key_disconnections.reasoning: This disconnection allows for the stereoselective formation of the key N,O-acetal ring system, which is central to the pyrrolizidine fragment of UCS1025A. It leverages the authors' novel iridium-catalyzed C-H functionalization strategy.

retrosynthetic_strategy.rationale: The strategy focuses on an intramolecular asymmetric propargylation of imides, specifically maleimide-tethered alkynes, to generate chiral N,O-acetals. This approach aims to establish the crucial stereochemistry of the pyrrolizidine fragment early in the synthesis, avoiding traditional chiral pool or resolution methods. The resulting N,O-acetal 4c is then transformed into known intermediate 7, completing a formal total synthesis.

thought.background: Esters are versatile but generally not used for stereocontrol in asymmetric synthesis due to bis-addition issues. Previous methods for acetal synthesis involve organocatalysis or transition metal catalysis, often focusing on C-heteroatom bond formation. The authors' group has prior experience with Ir-catalyzed C-H functionalization.
thought.gap: Direct, enantioselective addition of a carbon nucleophile into an ester to form acetals via stereoselective C-C bond formation is an unexplored retrosynthetic disconnection. The weak electrophilicity of esters and imides, combined with the need for mild conditions for acetal survival, presents a significant challenge.
thought.decision: To achieve stereoselective acetal formation, we will explore an iridium-catalyzed cyclization of ester-tethered alkynes. We hypothesize that complexation-assisted deprotonation at the propargylic position will generate an allenyliridium species, which will then undergo intramolecular nucleophilic attack on the ester carbonyl, activated by a Lewis acid (e.g., R3SiOTf), to form cyclic silyl acetals.

evidence.text_span:
Compared to previous approaches, the direct, enantioselective addition of a carbon nucleophile into an ester constitutes an unexplored retrosynthetic disconnection for the assembly of acetals. Monocyclic O,O- and N,O-acetals have previously been prepared using asymmetric organocatalysis and transition metal catalysis.4 For instance, in early work, List and coworkers reported an intramolecular transacetalisation protocol using a chiral phosphoric acid catalyst (Scheme 1B).5 In addition, spirocyclic and fused bicyclic O,O- and N,O-acetals have previously been prepared using a number of strategies that employ transition metal-catalysed cascade processes (Scheme 1C).6 For example, Slaughter and coworkers reported an early example in which alkyne activation by cationic Au was leveraged to generate an oxocarbenium species, which underwent subsequent stereoselective alcohol addition, while Ding reported a tandem hydrogenation/Brønsted acid mediated cyclisation employing an Ir catalyst that mediated both processes.7 In contrast to previous strategies, in which stereoselective C– heteroatom bond formation plays a crucial role, we report herein the asymmetric addition of tethered carbanion nucleophiles to esters to generate acetals through the stereoselective construction of C–C bonds (Scheme 1D).
Given the important role of chiral N- and O-containing heterocycles in drug development and natural product synthesis,8 and their potential utility as precursors to electrophiles that engage in cross coupling,9 we undertook the exploration of cyclisation reactions of alkynes containing tethered electrophilic coupling partners. Based on recent work in Au catalysis report by Zhang and coworkers on intramolecular aldehyde addition,10 as well as recent work in Fe- and Ircatalysed C–H functionalisation chemistry reported by our group,11 we considered the possibility of using simple esters as underexplored electrophiles for reaction with catalytically
generated organoiridium nucleophiles to afford O,O-acetals. In particular, we hypothesised that ester-tethered alkyne 1 could undergo complexation-assisted deprotonation at the propargylic position upon coordination to iridium complex II (Scheme 2),11 and that the ester group in the resultant allenyliridium species IV would then be susceptible to intramolecular nucleophilic attack upon activation of the carbonyl by a suitable Lewis acid (e.g., R3SiOTf) to form cyclic silyl acetal 2. Although conceptually similar to previously investigated reactivity, the weak inherent electrophilicity of the ester, combined with the mild reaction conditions required for the survival of the acetal product, renders the implementation of the proposed reactivity a signicant synthetic challenge.

thought.background: Initial hypothesis formed for Ir-catalyzed cyclization of ester-tethered alkynes.
thought.gap: Optimal conditions for the reaction need to be identified, particularly regarding the silyl source, base equivalents, catalyst loading, temperature, and solvent.
thought.decision: To optimize the reaction, we will examine model acetate substrate 1a using various electrophilic silyl sources under previously developed iridium-catalyzed enantioselective propargylic functionalization conditions (5 mol% [Ir(cod)Cl]2, 20 mol% Carreira's ligand (R)-L, and 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine (TMPH) as the base).

outcome.observation: The reaction proceeded with excellent yield and enantioselectivity. TIPSOTf was found to be the most effective silyl source.

evidence.text_span:
At the outset, we examined model acetate substrate 1a using various electrophilic silyl sources under conditions previously
developed by our group for iridium-catalysed enantioselective propargylic functionalisation (5 mol% [Ir(cod)Cl]2, 20 mol% Carreira's ligand (R)-L, and 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine (TMPH) as the base, Table 1). Among the Lewis acids examined, the bulky triisopropylsilyl triate (TIPSOTf) proved to be the most effective, while smaller silyl triates gave other silylation products as side products and were therefore less effective (entry 1 vs. 12 and 13).

thought.background: TIPSOTf was identified as the most effective silyl source for the model reaction.
thought.gap: Investigate the effect of different silyl triflates on the reaction outcome.
thought.decision: To explore the silyl source, we will test TBSOTf in place of TIPSOTf.

outcome.observation: TBSOTf resulted in lower yield and other silylation products as side products. Enantiomeric excess was not determined.

evidence.text_span: Among the Lewis acids examined, the bulky triisopropylsilyl triate (TIPSOTf) proved to be the most effective, while smaller silyl triates gave other silylation products as side products and were therefore less effective (entry 1 vs. 12 and 13).

thought.background: TIPSOTf was identified as the most effective silyl source for the model reaction.
thought.gap: Investigate the effect of different silyl triflates on the reaction outcome.
thought.decision: To explore the silyl source, we will test TESOTf in place of TIPSOTf.

outcome.observation: TESOTf resulted in very low yield and other silylation products as side products. Enantiomeric excess was not determined.

evidence.text_span: Among the Lewis acids examined, the bulky triisopropylsilyl triate (TIPSOTf) proved to be the most effective, while smaller silyl triates gave other silylation products as side products and were therefore less effective (entry 1 vs. 12 and 13).

thought.background: TIPSOTf was identified as the most effective silyl source for the model reaction.
thought.gap: Investigate the effect of different silyl sources on the reaction outcome.
thought.decision: To explore the silyl source, we will test TIPSCl in place of TIPSOTf.

outcome.observation: TIPSCl gave no product.

evidence.text_span: Among the Lewis acids examined, the bulky triisopropylsilyl triate (TIPSOTf) proved to be the most effective, while smaller silyl triates gave other silylation products as side products and were therefore less effective (entry 1 vs. 12 and 13).

thought.background: TBSOTf and TESOTf gave lower yields and side products, while TIPSCl gave no reaction.
thought.gap: The bulky TIPSOTf is crucial for high yield and selectivity. The initial conditions with TIPSOTf are optimal for the model substrate.
thought.decision: To proceed with the reaction, we will use TIPSOTf as the silyl source under the optimized conditions (5 mol% [Ir(cod)Cl]2, 20 mol% Carreira's ligand (R)-L, 2.5 equiv TMPH, 2 equiv TIPSOTf in DCE at 35 °C).

outcome.observation: TIPSOTf was confirmed as the most effective silyl source, leading to high yield and enantioselectivity for the model reaction.

evidence.text_span: Among the Lewis acids examined, the bulky triisopropylsilyl triate (TIPSOTf) proved to be the most effective, while smaller silyl triates gave other silylation products as side products and were therefore less effective (entry 1 vs. 12 and 13).

thought.background: The initial reaction conditions for model substrate 1a yielded 87% with 99% ee at 35 °C.
thought.gap: Investigate the effect of reaction temperature on yield and enantioselectivity.
thought.decision: To explore temperature effects, we will conduct the reaction at room temperature (21 °C).

outcome.observation: Lowering the temperature to 21 °C resulted in a decreased yield (73%) while maintaining high ee (98%).

evidence.text_span: The outcome (both enantioselectivity and reactivity) exhibited a marked dependence on the reaction temperature (entries 1–3), and in the exploration of scope, further adjustments to this parameter were made on an individual basis, depending on the steric and electronic properties of the substrate.

thought.background: The initial reaction conditions for model substrate 1a yielded 87% with 99% ee at 35 °C.
thought.gap: Investigate the effect of reaction temperature on yield and enantioselectivity.
thought.decision: To explore temperature effects, we will conduct the reaction at 40 °C.

outcome.observation: Increasing the temperature to 40 °C resulted in a slightly decreased ee (96%) while maintaining a good yield (86%).

evidence.text_span: The outcome (both enantioselectivity and reactivity) exhibited a marked dependence on the reaction temperature (entries 1–3), and in the exploration of scope, further adjustments to this parameter were made on an individual basis, depending on the steric and electronic properties of the substrate.

thought.background: Both lower (21 °C) and higher (40 °C) temperatures led to slightly suboptimal results compared to 35 °C.
thought.gap: The optimal temperature for the model reaction is 35 °C. However, temperature may need to be adjusted for different substrates.
thought.decision: To proceed, the reaction temperature will be maintained at 35 °C for the model substrate, with the understanding that it may be adjusted for other substrates based on their steric and electronic properties.

outcome.observation: 35 °C was confirmed as the optimal temperature for the model reaction, providing the best balance of yield and enantioselectivity. Temperature adjustments will be made on a case-by-case basis for other substrates.

evidence.text_span: The outcome (both enantioselectivity and reactivity) exhibited a marked dependence on the reaction temperature (entries 1–3), and in the exploration of scope, further adjustments to this parameter were made on an individual basis, depending on the steric and electronic properties of the substrate.

thought.background: The initial reaction conditions for model substrate 1a used 2.5 equiv TMPH and 2 equiv TIPSOTf.
thought.gap: Investigate the effect of varying the equivalents of TMPH and TIPSOTf on yield and enantioselectivity.
thought.decision: To explore stoichiometry, we will decrease the equivalents of both TMPH and TIPSOTf to 1.5 equiv each.

outcome.observation: Decreasing the equivalents of TMPH and TIPSOTf to 1.5 equiv each resulted in a slight decrease in yield (82%) and ee (97%).

evidence.text_span: Decreasing the number of equivalents of TMPH and TIPSOTf simultaneously (entry 4), or increasing the excess of TMPH relative to TIPSOTf (entry 5) were both marginally detrimental to yield and enantiomeric excess (ee).

thought.background: The initial reaction conditions for model substrate 1a used 2.5 equiv TMPH and 2 equiv TIPSOTf.
thought.gap: Investigate the effect of varying the equivalents of TMPH and TIPSOTf on yield and enantioselectivity.
thought.decision: To explore stoichiometry, we will increase the excess of TMPH relative to TIPSOTf (3 equiv TMPH, 2 equiv TIPSOTf).

outcome.observation: Increasing the excess of TMPH relative to TIPSOTf resulted in a slight decrease in yield (83%) and ee (96%).

evidence.text_span: Decreasing the number of equivalents of TMPH and TIPSOTf simultaneously (entry 4), or increasing the excess of TMPH relative to TIPSOTf (entry 5) were both marginally detrimental to yield and enantiomeric excess (ee).

thought.background: Varying the equivalents of TMPH and TIPSOTf from the initial conditions (2.5 equiv TMPH, 2 equiv TIPSOTf) led to marginally detrimental effects on yield and ee.
thought.gap: The initial stoichiometry appears optimal for the model reaction.
thought.decision: To proceed, the stoichiometry of TMPH and TIPSOTf will be maintained at 2.5 equiv and 2 equiv, respectively.

outcome.observation: The initial stoichiometry of 2.5 equiv TMPH and 2 equiv TIPSOTf was confirmed as optimal for the model reaction.

evidence.text_span: Decreasing the number of equivalents of TMPH and TIPSOTf simultaneously (entry 4), or increasing the excess of TMPH relative to TIPSOTf (entry 5) were both marginally detrimental to yield and enantiomeric excess (ee).

thought.background: The initial reaction conditions for model substrate 1a used 5 mol% [Ir(cod)Cl]2.
thought.gap: Investigate the effect of catalyst loading on yield and enantioselectivity.
thought.decision: To explore catalyst loading, we will reduce it to 2.5 mol% [Ir(cod)Cl]2.

outcome.observation: Reducing the catalyst loading to 2.5 mol% resulted in a lower yield (70%) but still useful, with high ee (97%).

evidence.text_span: Conducting the reaction at half of the standard catalyst loading resulted in lower, but still useful, yield (entry 6).

thought.background: Reducing catalyst loading to 2.5 mol% resulted in a lower but still useful yield.
thought.gap: For optimal yield, the higher catalyst loading (5 mol%) is preferred, but lower loading can be used if yield is acceptable.
thought.decision: To proceed with optimal conditions, the catalyst loading will be maintained at 5 mol% for the model reaction, but the possibility of using lower loading for specific substrates will be considered.

outcome.observation: 5 mol% [Ir(cod)Cl]2 is preferred for optimal yield, but 2.5 mol% can be used if a slightly lower yield is acceptable.

evidence.text_span: Conducting the reaction at half of the standard catalyst loading resulted in lower, but still useful, yield (entry 6).

thought.background: The initial reaction conditions for model substrate 1a used 1,2-dichloroethane (DCE) as solvent.
thought.gap: Investigate the effect of different solvents on yield and enantioselectivity.
thought.decision: To explore solvent effects, we will test CH2Cl2 as solvent.

outcome.observation: CH2Cl2 as solvent resulted in a slightly lower yield (83%) and ee (96%) compared to DCE.

evidence.text_span: Finally, 1,2-dichloroethane (DCE) was slightly preferable to CH2Cl2 as the solvent; however, both were far superior to a range of aromatic solvents investigated (entries 7–11).

thought.background: DCE was slightly preferable to CH2Cl2 as solvent.
thought.gap: Investigate the effect of aromatic solvents on yield and enantioselectivity.
thought.decision: To explore solvent effects, we will test o-dichlorobenzene as solvent.

outcome.observation: o-Dichlorobenzene as solvent resulted in significantly lower yield (55%) and ee (92%).

evidence.text_span: Finally, 1,2-dichloroethane (DCE) was slightly preferable to CH2Cl2 as the solvent; however, both were far superior to a range of aromatic solvents investigated (entries 7–11).

thought.background: DCE was slightly preferable to CH2Cl2 as solvent, and aromatic solvents were generally inferior.
thought.gap: Investigate the effect of other aromatic solvents on yield and enantioselectivity.
thought.decision: To explore solvent effects, we will test chlorobenzene as solvent.

outcome.observation: Chlorobenzene as solvent resulted in very low yield (12%). Enantiomeric excess was not determined.

evidence.text_span: Finally, 1,2-dichloroethane (DCE) was slightly preferable to CH2Cl2 as the solvent; however, both were far superior to a range of aromatic solvents investigated (entries 7–11).

thought.background: DCE was slightly preferable to CH2Cl2 as solvent, and aromatic solvents were generally inferior.
thought.gap: Investigate the effect of other aromatic solvents on yield and enantioselectivity.
thought.decision: To explore solvent effects, we will test PhCF3 as solvent.

outcome.observation: PhCF3 as solvent resulted in very low yield (5%). Enantiomeric excess was not determined.

evidence.text_span: Finally, 1,2-dichloroethane (DCE) was slightly preferable to CH2Cl2 as the solvent; however, both were far superior to a range of aromatic solvents investigated (entries 7–11).

thought.background: DCE was slightly preferable to CH2Cl2 as solvent, and aromatic solvents were generally inferior.
thought.gap: Investigate the effect of other aromatic solvents on yield and enantioselectivity.
thought.decision: To explore solvent effects, we will test toluene as solvent.

outcome.observation: Toluene as solvent resulted in only trace product. Enantiomeric excess was not determined.

evidence.text_span: Finally, 1,2-dichloroethane (DCE) was slightly preferable to CH2Cl2 as the solvent; however, both were far superior to a range of aromatic solvents investigated (entries 7–11).

thought.background: DCE was slightly preferable to CH2Cl2, and aromatic solvents (o-dichlorobenzene, chlorobenzene, PhCF3, toluene) were significantly inferior or led to no reaction.
thought.gap: DCE is the optimal solvent for the model reaction.
thought.decision: To proceed, 1,2-dichloroethane (DCE) will be used as the solvent.

outcome.observation: 1,2-dichloroethane (DCE) was confirmed as the optimal solvent for the model reaction, being superior to CH2Cl2 and significantly better than aromatic solvents.

evidence.text_span: Finally, 1,2-dichloroethane (DCE) was slightly preferable to CH2Cl2 as the solvent; however, both were far superior to a range of aromatic solvents investigated (entries 7–11).

thought.background: Optimized conditions for ester cyclization were established using model substrate 1a.
thought.gap: The substrate scope for ester cyclization needs to be explored, including different ester types and ring sizes, and the optimal temperature for each.
thought.decision: To explore the substrate scope, various ester substrates will be subjected to the optimized iridium-catalyzed cyclization conditions, with temperature adjustments made on an individual basis.

outcome.observation: The acetate substrate 1a successfully formed the 5-membered O,O-acetal 2a with excellent yield, ee, and dr. 50 °C was found to be optimal for most cases.

evidence.text_span:
For ve membered ring formation, this strategy proved applicable to a variety of esters, with 50 °C giving optimal results in most cases (Scheme 3). In addition to the original acetate substrate, a formate ester was used successfully to form
unsubstituted acetal 2b. Similarly, a benzoate ester (2d) and 4- methoxybenzoate ester (2j) were also successful under these conditions. Furthermore, homologous substrates could be employed to provide the corresponding tetrahydropyrans 2c, 2g and 2h.

thought.background: The optimized conditions for ester cyclization were applied to the substrate scope.
thought.gap: Confirm the applicability of the method to a formate ester to form an unsubstituted acetal.
thought.decision: To expand the scope, a formate ester will be subjected to the optimized iridium-catalyzed cyclization conditions.

outcome.observation: A formate ester successfully formed the unsubstituted acetal 2b with good yield, excellent ee, and good dr.

evidence.text_span:
For ve membered ring formation, this strategy proved applicable to a variety of esters, with 50 °C giving optimal results in most cases (Scheme 3). In addition to the original acetate substrate, a formate ester was used successfully to form
unsubstituted acetal 2b.

thought.background: The optimized conditions for ester cyclization were applied to the substrate scope.
thought.gap: Confirm the applicability of the method to a benzoate ester.
thought.decision: To expand the scope, a benzoate ester will be subjected to the optimized iridium-catalyzed cyclization conditions.

outcome.observation: A benzoate ester successfully formed the acetal 2d with good yield, excellent ee, and good dr.

evidence.text_span: Similarly, a benzoate ester (2d) and 4- methoxybenzoate ester (2j) were also successful under these conditions.

thought.background: The optimized conditions for ester cyclization were applied to the substrate scope.
thought.gap: Confirm the applicability of the method to a 4-methoxybenzoate ester.
thought.decision: To expand the scope, a 4-methoxybenzoate ester will be subjected to the optimized iridium-catalyzed cyclization conditions.

outcome.observation: A 4-methoxybenzoate ester successfully formed the acetal 2j with good yield, excellent ee, and good dr.

evidence.text_span: Similarly, a benzoate ester (2d) and 4- methoxybenzoate ester (2j) were also successful under these conditions.

thought.background: The optimized conditions for ester cyclization were applied to the substrate scope.
thought.gap: Confirm the applicability of the method to homologous substrates to provide tetrahydropyrans.
thought.decision: To expand the scope, homologous substrates will be subjected to the optimized iridium-catalyzed cyclization conditions to form 6-membered rings.

outcome.observation: A homologous acetate substrate successfully formed the 6-membered O,O-acetal 2c with good yield, excellent ee, but moderate dr. The relative configuration was syn.

evidence.text_span:
Furthermore, homologous substrates could be employed to provide the corresponding tetrahydropyrans 2c, 2g and 2h.
Interestingly, based on analysis of 1H NMR coupling constants, the relative conguration of the alkynyl and silyloxy groups of 2c was assigned as syn, rather than the anti-conguration assigned to 2g and 2h, as well as 2b and all analogous ve-membered O,O-acetal products (see the ESI† for details for the stereochemical assignments).

thought.background: The optimized conditions for ester cyclization were applied to the substrate scope.
thought.gap: Confirm the applicability of the method to homologous substrates to provide tetrahydropyrans.
thought.decision: To expand the scope, homologous substrates will be subjected to the optimized iridium-catalyzed cyclization conditions to form 6-membered rings.

outcome.observation: A homologous formate substrate successfully formed the 6-membered O,O-acetal 2g with good yield, excellent ee, and good dr. The relative configuration was anti.

evidence.text_span:
Furthermore, homologous substrates could be employed to provide the corresponding tetrahydropyrans 2c, 2g and 2h.
Interestingly, based on analysis of 1H NMR coupling constants, the relative conguration of the alkynyl and silyloxy groups of 2c was assigned as syn, rather than the anti-conguration assigned to 2g and 2h, as well as 2b and all analogous ve-membered O,O-acetal products (see the ESI† for details for the stereochemical assignments).

thought.background: The optimized conditions for ester cyclization were applied to the substrate scope.
thought.gap: Confirm the applicability of the method to homologous substrates to provide tetrahydropyrans.
thought.decision: To expand the scope, homologous substrates will be subjected to the optimized iridium-catalyzed cyclization conditions to form 6-membered rings.

outcome.observation: A homologous benzoate substrate successfully formed the 6-membered O,O-acetal 2h with good yield, excellent ee, and good dr. The relative configuration was anti. Diastereomers were inseparable.

evidence.text_span:
Furthermore, homologous substrates could be employed to provide the corresponding tetrahydropyrans 2c, 2g and 2h.
Interestingly, based on analysis of 1H NMR coupling constants, the relative conguration of the alkynyl and silyloxy groups of 2c was assigned as syn, rather than the anti-conguration assigned to 2g and 2h, as well as 2b and all analogous ve-membered O,O-acetal products (see the ESI† for details for the stereochemical assignments).

thought.background: The optimized conditions for ester cyclization were applied to the substrate scope.
thought.gap: Confirm the applicability of the method to benzoate esters bearing moderate electron donors on the alkynyl aryl group.
thought.decision: To expand the scope, a benzoate ester with a methyl group on the alkynyl aryl group will be subjected to the optimized iridium-catalyzed cyclization conditions.

outcome.observation: Benzoate ester 2e bearing a moderate electron donor on the alkynyl aryl group was tolerated, but cyclized slowly, requiring 3 days for good yield, excellent ee, and good dr.

evidence.text_span: Benzoate ester 2e bearing a moderate electron donor on the alkynyl aryl group was tolerated, but cyclised slowly and needed a reaction time of 3 days.

thought.background: The optimized conditions for ester cyclization were applied to the substrate scope.
thought.gap: Confirm the applicability of the method to benzoate esters bearing moderate to strong electron-withdrawing groups on the alkynyl aryl group.
thought.decision: To expand the scope, a benzoate ester with a nitro group on the alkynyl aryl group will be subjected to the optimized iridium-catalyzed cyclization conditions.

outcome.observation: Benzoate ester 2f with a nitro group on the alkynyl aryl group was tolerated, giving good yield, excellent ee, and good dr.

evidence.text_span: Moderate to strong electron withdrawing groups were also tolerated on this ring (2f, 2i, and 2k), though in the case of 2i, optimal yield required an extended reaction time and additional catalyst.

thought.background: The optimized conditions for ester cyclization were applied to the substrate scope.
thought.gap: Confirm the applicability of the method to benzoate esters bearing moderate to strong electron-withdrawing groups on the alkynyl aryl group, specifically a chlorinated pyridine.
thought.decision: To expand the scope, a benzoate ester with a chlorinated pyridine on the alkynyl aryl group will be subjected to the iridium-catalyzed cyclization conditions, with adjustments for optimal yield.

outcome.observation: Benzoate ester 2i with a chlorinated pyridine on the alkynyl aryl group was tolerated, but required extended reaction time (36h) and additional catalyst (10 mol% [Ir(cod)Cl]2, 40 mol% L) for optimal yield, while maintaining excellent ee and good dr.

evidence.text_span: Moderate to strong electron withdrawing groups were also tolerated on this ring (2f, 2i, and 2k), though in the case of 2i, optimal yield required an extended reaction time and additional catalyst.

thought.background: The optimized conditions for ester cyclization were applied to the substrate scope.
thought.gap: Confirm the applicability of the method to a pivalate ester, which is bulky.
thought.decision: To expand the scope, a pivalate ester will be subjected to the iridium-catalyzed cyclization conditions. Given the bulkiness, TBSOTf might be a better silyl source than TIPSOTf.

outcome.observation: A tethered pivalate underwent addition to give t-butyl substituted product 2k. TBSOTf was used in place of TIPSOTf, as the latter gave low conversion due to steric crowding. Good yield, excellent ee, and good dr were obtained.

evidence.text_span: A tethered pivalate underwent addition to give tbutyl substituted product 2k. In this example tert-butyldimethylsilyl triate (TBSOTf) was used in place of TIPSOTf, as the latter gave low conversion of the starting material. This was presumed to be the result of crowding between the bulky acyl group and the silyl protecting group.

thought.background: The iridium-catalyzed cyclization was successful for various esters. The next step is to explore nitrogen-tethered substrates for N,O-acetal formation.
thought.gap: Amides were found to be insufficiently electrophilic. Imides need to be tested for their reactivity and selectivity.
thought.decision: To generate N,O-acetals, phthalimide-tethered alkynes will be subjected to the iridium-catalyzed cyclization conditions. Smaller silyl groups (TMS, TBS) will be tested as they might be more effective than TIPS for imides.

outcome.observation: Phthalimide-tethered alkynes successfully cyclized to give fused bicyclic N,O-acetals (e.g., 4a) with good yield, excellent ee, and excellent dr. TMSOTf was used as the silyl triflate.

evidence.text_span: We then turned to nitrogen-tethered substrates for the generation of N,O-acetals. We found that while amides appeared insufficiently electrophilic to participate in the reaction, substrates bearing imides could be cyclised to give silylprotected hemiaminals. Phthalimide- and maleimide-tethered alkynes gave rise to fused bi- and tricyclic products, which were also isolable as single diastereomers (Scheme 3). These cyclisation reactions exhibited optimal yield when the smaller TMS and TBS groups were used in place of TIPS, while maintaining excellent ee and dr.

thought.background: The iridium-catalyzed cyclization was successful for phthalimide-tethered alkynes using TMSOTf.
thought.gap: Confirm the applicability of the method to maleimide-tethered alkynes, which are relevant for UCS1025A synthesis.
thought.decision: To generate N,O-acetals, maleimide-tethered alkynes will be subjected to the iridium-catalyzed cyclization conditions. TBSOTf will be tested as the silyl source.

outcome.observation: Maleimide-tethered alkyne 3c successfully cyclized to give the tricyclic N,O-acetal 4c with good yield, excellent ee, and excellent dr. TBSOTf was used as the silyl triflate.

evidence.text_span: We then turned to nitrogen-tethered substrates for the generation of N,O-acetals. We found that while amides appeared insufficiently electrophilic to participate in the reaction, substrates bearing imides could be cyclised to give silylprotected hemiaminals. Phthalimide- and maleimide-tethered alkynes gave rise to fused bi- and tricyclic products, which were also isolable as single diastereomers (Scheme 3). These cyclisation reactions exhibited optimal yield when the smaller TMS and TBS groups were used in place of TIPS, while maintaining excellent ee and dr.

thought.background: Maleimide-tethered alkyne 3c successfully cyclized to 4c. This product is structurally similar to intermediates for UCS1025A.
thought.gap: The synthesis of 3c needs to be efficient and high-yielding.
thought.decision: To prepare 3c, modified Mitsunobu conditions will be used, specifically with neopentyl alcohol as an additive, as reported by Walker.

outcome.observation: Maleimide-tethered alkyne 3c was prepared in 96% yield using modified Mitsunobu conditions with neopentyl alcohol as an additive.

evidence.text_span: Maleimide-tethered alkyne 3c was prepared in 96% yield using modied Mitsunobu conditions rst reported by Walker.15 In particular, the addition of neopentyl alcohol as an additive was critical 96% yield vs. <10% yield without the additive).

thought.background: Maleimide-tethered alkyne 3c was prepared using modified Mitsunobu conditions with neopentyl alcohol as an additive.
thought.gap: Confirm the importance of the neopentyl alcohol additive.
thought.decision: To confirm the role of the additive, the Mitsunobu reaction will be attempted without neopentyl alcohol.

outcome.observation: Without neopentyl alcohol, the yield of 3c was less than 10%.

evidence.text_span: Maleimide-tethered alkyne 3c was prepared in 96% yield using modied Mitsunobu conditions rst reported by Walker.15 In particular, the addition of neopentyl alcohol as an additive was critical 96% yield vs. <10% yield without the additive).

thought.background: The Mitsunobu reaction without neopentyl alcohol gave very low yield.
thought.gap: The neopentyl alcohol additive is critical for high yield in the synthesis of 3c.
thought.decision: To ensure high yield, neopentyl alcohol will always be included as an additive in the Mitsunobu reaction for 3c synthesis.

outcome.observation: Neopentyl alcohol is a critical additive for the high-yielding synthesis of 3c via modified Mitsunobu conditions.

evidence.text_span: Maleimide-tethered alkyne 3c was prepared in 96% yield using modied Mitsunobu conditions rst reported by Walker.15 In particular, the addition of neopentyl alcohol as an additive was critical 96% yield vs. <10% yield without the additive).

thought.background: Maleimide-tethered alkyne 3c was successfully cyclized to 4c (step 33). The next step is to remove the extraneous aryl substituent from 4c.
thought.gap: A method for selective semi-hydrogenation of the alkyne to a cis-alkene is needed.
thought.decision: To remove the aryl substituent, alkyne 4c will be subjected to semi-hydrogenation conditions using Lindlar Pd and quinoline.

outcome.observation: Semi-hydrogenation of 4c afforded cis-configured alkene 5 in 88% yield.

evidence.text_span: To remove the extraneous aryl substituent, alkyne 4c was rst subjected to semi-hydrogenation conditions to afford ciscongured alkene 5 in 88% yield.

thought.background: Alkene 5 was obtained from 4c via semi-hydrogenation. The next step is to convert the alkene to a carboxylic acid.
thought.gap: A two-step sequence involving ozonolysis and Pinnick oxidation is required to achieve this transformation.
thought.decision: To obtain carboxylic acid 6, alkene 5 will undergo ozonolysis followed by Pinnick oxidation of the resulting aldehyde.

outcome.observation: Ozonolysis followed by Pinnick oxidation of 5 gave carboxylic acid 6 in 48% yield over 2 steps.

evidence.text_span: Ozonolysis followed by Pinnick oxidation of the resulting aldehyde then gave carboxylic acid 6 in 48% yield over 2 steps.

thought.background: Carboxylic acid 6 was obtained from 5. The final step to reach intermediate 7 is iodolactonization.
thought.gap: A method for iodolactonization of 6 to form iodide 7 is needed, ensuring no erosion of stereochemical purity.
thought.decision: To obtain iodide 7, carboxylic acid 6 will undergo iodolactonization using I2 and NaHCO3.

outcome.observation: Iodolactonization of 6 provided iodide 7 in 91% yield without erosion of stereochemical purity (99% ee). This completes the formal total synthesis of UCS1025A.

evidence.text_span: Finally, iodolactonisation provided iodide 7 in 91% yield 23% over 6 steps) without erosion of stereochemical purity (99% ee). This compound, whose spectral data were in accord with those previously reported by Danishefsky and Lambert, had previously been converted to UCS1025A in three additional steps.13a Since the requisite aldehyde coupling partner for the Danishefsky synthesis can be prepared in enantioenriched form in six steps from commercial materials, the preparation of 7 as described above constitutes a nine-step (longest linear sequence) formal total synthesis of this natural product.16

Overall Yield.interpretation: Overall yield for the 6-step sequence to intermediate 7.

Longest Linear Steps.interpretation: Longest linear sequence for the formal total synthesis of UCS1025A, including preparation of starting materials.

validation.comparison_to_prior: The current approach allows for the use of simple and accessible starting materials without the need to discard an unwanted stereoisomer, unlike previous chiral pool and chiral resolution strategies. It also applies catalytic enantioselective catalysis to the key furopyrrolizidine ring system, which was not done in prior syntheses.
validation.significance: The developed method provides an efficient and stereoselective route to cyclic silyl O,O- and N,O-acetals via propargylic C–H functionalization. Its application to the formal total synthesis of UCS1025A demonstrates a novel strategy for establishing stereochemistry in complex natural products, offering advantages over traditional methods by avoiding chiral pool or resolution and utilizing catalytic enantioselective catalysis.

source.title: Intramolecular asymmetric propargylation of esters and imides: C-H functionalisation enables stereocontrolled access to UCS1025A.
source.authors: Corcoran JC, Zhu J, Semenick MA, Welser AL, Wang YM.
